endations and actions. If these 


[Page 14]
knowledge assets are not maintained properly, AI quality will inevitably decline. Retrieval evaluation should therefore be viewed as 
part of a broader knowledge management strategy rather than a purely technical activity. 
Evaluating retrieval quality requires appropriate metrics. Traditionally, information retrieval systems have used measures such as 
precision and recall. Precision focuses on how many retrieved items are relevant, while recall measures how much relevant 
information was successfully retrieved. Although these concepts remain valuable, modern RAG systems often require additional 
evaluation dimensions. Organisations increasingly assess groundedness, context relevance, answer relevance, faithfulness, 
completeness, and user satisfaction to obtain a more comprehensive understanding of system performance. 
A growing ecosystem of evaluation frameworks has emerged to support these activities. Tools such as TruLens, RAGAS, and LangSmith 
provide mechanisms for measuring retrieval and generation quality. These frameworks help organisations evaluate whether retrieved 
context was relevant, whether answers were supported by evid

chunks.source_document: Enterprise AI Consulting & Production Readiness Handbook.pdf
chunks.source_text_file: Enterprise AI Consulting & Production Readiness Handbook.txt
chunks.text:
ngSmith 
provide mechanisms for measuring retrieval and generation quality. These frameworks help organisations evaluate whether retrieved 
context was relevant, whether answers were supported by evidence, and whether responses addressed user intent effectively. While 
automated evaluation is valuable, it should not be viewed as a complete replacement for human judgement. Human evaluation 
remains essential because many aspects of quality involve context, domain expertise, and user expectations that cannot be fully 
captured through automated metrics. 
Human evaluation is particularly important when assessing real-world usefulness. A response may achieve strong technical evaluation 
scores while still failing to satisfy users. For example, an answer may be factually correct but overly complex, difficult to understand, 
or insufficiently actionable. Human reviewers provide insight into these practical considerations and help ensure that evaluation 
remains aligned with business objectives. 
Another important consideration is continuous evaluation. Many organisations treat evaluation as a one-time activity performed 
before deployment. This approach is insufficient for enterprise env

chunks.text:
es. 
Another important consideration is continuous evaluation. Many organisations treat evaluation as a one-time activity performed 
before deployment. This approach is insufficient for enterprise environments where knowledge, user behaviour, regulations, and 
business requirements evolve continuously. Retrieval quality can deteriorate over time as new documents are added, content changes, 
or user expectations shift. Continuous monitoring enables organisations to identify issues early and maintain confidence in system 
performance. 
The flight booking platform demonstrates the value of continuous evaluation. Airline schedules change, pricing structures evolve, 
travel restrictions are updated, and corporate policies are revised. A retrieval system that performs well today may become less 
effective tomorrow if knowledge assets are not updated appropriately. Continuous evaluation ensures that changes in content and 
usage patterns are reflected in system performance assessments. 
An important lesson for enterprise architects is that RAG quality cannot be achieved through technology alone. Retrieval engines, 
vector databases, embeddings models, rerankers, and language models all co

chunks.source_document: Enterprise AI Consulting & Production Readiness Handbook.pdf
chunks.source_text_file: Enterprise AI Consulting & Production Readiness Handbook.txt
chunks.text:
portant lesson for enterprise architects is that RAG quality cannot be achieved through technology alone. Retrieval engines, 
vector databases, embeddings models, rerankers, and language models all contribute to system performance, but none can 
compensate fully for poor knowledge management or inadequate evaluation practices. High-quality RAG systems emerge when 
retrieval, knowledge governance, evaluation, and operational monitoring work together as a coherent capability. 
From a consulting perspective, RAG evaluation is often overlooked because stakeholders focus on demonstrations and visible outputs. 
During pilot phases, organisations may be impressed by convincing responses without fully understanding whether those responses 
are consistently accurate. Consultants play an important role in shifting attention from isolated examples to measurable performance. 
The objective is not to demonstrate that the system can answer some questions correctly; it is to demonstrate that it can answer 
important questions reliably, consistently, and transparently. 
Interviewers frequently explore this topic because it reveals whether candidates understand enterprise AI beyond basic 
implement

nswer 
important questions reliably, consistently, and transparently. 
Interviewers frequently explore this topic because it reveals whether candidates understand enterprise AI beyond basic 
implementation. Questions often focus on how hallucinations can be reduced, how retrieval quality can be measured, how knowledge 
quality influences outcomes, and how organisations should evaluate RAG systems in production. Strong answers recognise that 
retrieval and generation are separate but interconnected components and emphasise the importance of evidence-based evaluation 
rather than relying solely on model performance. 
Ultimately, Retrieval-Augmented Generation is not simply a retrieval mechanism or a prompting technique. It is an information 
delivery system that connects organisational knowledge with AI reasoning. Its effectiveness depends on the quality of the knowledge 
being managed, the relevance of the information being retrieved, the groundedness of generated responses, and the robustness of 
the evaluation framework used to measure performance. 
Key Takeaway 
The quality of a RAG system depends as much on retrieval quality and knowledge quality as it does on model quality. Eff

ustness of 
the evaluation framework used to measure performance. 
Key Takeaway 
The quality of a RAG system depends as much on retrieval quality and knowledge quality as it does on model quality. Effective 
enterprise RAG requires continuous evaluation of context relevance, answer relevance, groundedness, hallucination rates, and user 
satisfaction. Successful organisations treat retrieval evaluation as an ongoing operational discipline that combines automated metrics, 
human review, knowledge governance, and continuous monitoring to ensure that AI responses remain accurate, trustworthy, and 
business-relevant.

Chapter 7 – Human Evaluation, Adoption & Feedback Systems 
Version 2 Narrative Edition 
Many organisations invest significant effort in selecting models, building retrieval systems, designing agents, and deploying AI 
platforms. Yet despite technical success, some AI initiatives fail to deliver meaningful business value. The reason is often surprisingly 
simple: people do not use the system. Enterprise AI success is determined not only by technical performance but also by human 
adoption. An AI system that achieves exceptional evaluation scores but is ign

le: people do not use the system. Enterprise AI success is determined not only by technical performance but also by human 
adoption. An AI system that achieves exceptional evaluation scores but is ignored by users creates little value. Conversely, a system 
with modest technical sophistication may generate substantial business impact if users trust it, adopt it, and integrate it into their 
daily activities. 
This reality highlights the importance of human evaluation, adoption, and feedback systems. While technical evaluation focuses on 
metrics such as accuracy, latency, groundedness, and retrieval quality, human evaluation focuses on how people experience the 
system. Do users trust the recommendations? Do they understand the outputs? Does the system help them accomplish their 
objectives? Do they return and use it again? These questions ultimately determine whether an AI initiative succeeds in practice. 
A useful principle is that AI systems create value only when humans change their behaviour because of them. Generating 
recommendations is not enough. The recommendations must be accepted, trusted, and acted upon. This means that adoption should 
be considered from the earliest

r behaviour because of them. Generating 
recommendations is not enough. The recommendations must be accepted, trusted, and acted upon. This means that adoption should 
be considered from the earliest stages of design rather than being treated as an afterthought once deployment is complete. 
Trust plays a central role in adoption. Users are unlikely to rely on a system if they do not believe its outputs are accurate, reliable, or 
understandable. Trust develops gradually through consistent experiences. When recommendations prove useful, users become more 
confident. When explanations are clear, users become more comfortable. When the system behaves predictably, users become more 
willing to incorporate it into decision-making processes. Conversely, inconsistent outputs, unexplained recommendations, or visible 
errors can quickly undermine confidence. 
The flight booking platform provides a useful example. A traveller may initially use the system out of curiosity. If the platform 
consistently recommends appropriate routes, identifies cost-saving opportunities, and provides useful explanations, the traveller 
becomes increasingly willing to rely on its recommendations. Over time, tru

tently recommends appropriate routes, identifies cost-saving opportunities, and provides useful explanations, the traveller 
becomes increasingly willing to rely on its recommendations. Over time, trust transforms occasional usage into habitual usage. This 
behavioural shift is ultimately more important than any individual technical metric because it reflects real business value. 
The same principle applies to the NHS appointment discovery platform. Patients may initially approach AI-generated appointment 
recommendations with caution. However, if the platform consistently identifies suitable appointments, provides accurate information, 
and helps reduce waiting times, users begin to trust the service. Adoption grows because the system demonstrably improves outcomes 
rather than simply showcasing technology. 
Human evaluation seeks to measure these experiences systematically. Traditional software testing focuses on whether a feature works. 
Human evaluation focuses on whether a feature helps. This distinction is important because a technically correct system may still fail 
to deliver a satisfactory user experience. A recommendation may be accurate but difficult to understand. An a

to deliver a satisfactory user experience. A recommendation may be accurate but difficult to understand. An answer may be complete 
but overwhelming. A workflow may function correctly but require too much effort from the user. Human evaluation helps identify 
these issues and ensures that optimisation efforts remain aligned with user needs. 
One of the most direct forms of human evaluation is explicit feedback. Explicit feedback occurs when users actively express their 
opinions about the system. Examples include satisfaction ratings, thumbs-up and thumbs-down indicators, surveys, comments, 
reviews, and structured evaluations. These mechanisms provide valuable insight because they capture user perceptions directly. 
Organisations can identify patterns, detect dissatisfaction, and prioritise improvements based on real experiences. 
However, explicit feedback has limitations. Many users never provide ratings or comments. Others may provide feedback only when 
experiences are exceptionally positive or exceptionally negative. For this reason, organisations should complement explicit feedback

Implicit feedback is derived from user behaviour rather than direct statements. Examples include frequency of use, session duration, 
repeated interactions, acceptance of recommendations, abandonment rates, and workflow completion rates. These behavioural 
indicators often reveal whether users find a system genuinely useful. A platform that users return to repeatedly is likely creating value, 
even if explicit feedback is limited. 
Consider the train ticketing platform designed to reduce congestion and optimise passenger distribution. If passengers consistently 
accept alternative route recommendations, this behaviour provides strong evidence that the recommendations are useful. Conversely, 
if passengers frequently ignore suggestions and choose their original routes, the system may need improvement regardless of how 
technically sophisticated the recommendation engine appears.

Adoption should also be viewed as a journey rather than a single event. New technologies often follow a predictable pattern. Early 
adopters embrace innovation quickly. Other users remain cautious until benefits become clear. Some groups may actively resist 
change because existing processes are familiar and comfortable. Successful organisations recognise these dynamics and develop 
adoption strategies that support users throughout the transition. 
Education and communication play important roles in this process. Users are more likely to adopt AI systems when they understand 
what the technology does, how it works, and what limitations exist. Clear communication reduces uncertainty and helps establish 
realistic expectations. Organisations that fail to communicate effectively often encounter resistance because users fear losing control, 
being replaced, or relying on technology they do not fully understand.

they do need confidence that the platform operates responsibly and provides value. Effective onboarding, training, and

Another important aspect of human evaluation is measuring business outcomes. User satisfaction alone is insufficient. Organisations 
must understand whether adoption leads to measurable improvements. Depending on the use case, these improvements may include 
reduced operational costs, improved productivity, faster decision making, increased customer satisfaction, higher revenue, or reduced

but the organisation should also examine outcomes such as booking conversion rat

A/B testing provides another powerful mechanism for evaluating adoption and user behaviour. Rather than relying on assumptions, 
organisations can compare alternative designs, prompts, workflows, recommendation strategies, or user experiences. By observing 
real-world behaviour, teams gain evidence regarding which approach performs best. This evidence-driven methodology reduces 
uncertainty and supports continuous improvement. 
Continuous improvement is a critical component of successful adoption programmes. User expectations evolve. Business 
requirements change. New capabilities emerge. As a result, adoption cannot be treated as a one-time objective achieved at launch. 
Organisations must continuously monitor feedback, analyse behaviour, evaluate outcomes, and refine the system accordingly. The 
most successful AI platf

Organisations must continuously monitor feedback, analyse behaviour, evaluate outcomes, and refine the system accordingly. The

This feedback loop creates a virtuous cycle. User interactions generate insights. Insights inform improvements. Improvements enhance 
user experience. Better experiences increase adoption. Increased adoption generates additional feedback. Over time, the system 
becomes increasingly aligned with user needs and organisational objectives. This continuous learning process is one of the defining 
characteristics of mature AI products. 
An important lesson for enterprise architects is that technical quality and human adoption are related but distinct. A highly accurate 
system may fail if users do not trust it. Similarly, a trusted system may create risk if its outputs are not sufficiently reliable. 
Organisations must therefore optimise both dimensions simultaneously. Technical evaluation ensures that the system performs 
correctly, while human evaluation ensures that people are willing to use it.

From a consulting perspective, adoption is often the true measure of success. Clients rarely invest in AI because they want

technology into their daily activities. Consultants therefore focus not only on architecture and implementation but also on change

Key Takeaway 
The success of an AI system is determined not only by technical performance but also by human adoption. Organisations must evaluate 
trust, usability, engagement, feedback, and business outcomes alongside traditional technical metrics. Effective feedback loops, 
continuous improvement, user education, and evidence-based evaluation help ensure that AI systems are not only accurate and 
reliable but also trusted, adopted, and capable of delivering sustainable business value.

Version 2 Narrative Edition 
As organisations move beyond experimentation and begin deploying AI solutions at enterprise scale, architecture becomes 
increasingly important. Early prototy

As organisations move beyond experimentation and begin deploying AI solutions at enterprise scale, architecture becomes 
increasingly important. Early prototypes often focus on proving technical feasibility, demonstrating model capabilities, or validating 
business concepts. Production environments introduce a different set of challenges. Systems must support thousands of users, 
integrate with existing business applications, protect sensitive information, comply with regulatory requirements, operate reliably, 
and evolve continuously over time. These requirements demand a structured architectural approach rather than isolated AI 
components. 
Azure Enterprise AI Architecture provides a framework for building scalable, secure, governed, and operationally sustainable AI 
solutions. While individual Azure services are important, enterprise architecture is not primarily about selecting cloud products. It is 
about organising capabilities into logical layers that collectively support business objectives. A mature architecture separates concerns, 
establishes governance boundaries, and ensures that each component performs a clearly defined role

chunks.source_document: Enterprise AI Consulting & Production Readiness Handbook.pdf

cal layers that collectively support business objectives. A mature architecture separates concerns, 
establishes governance boundaries, and ensures that each component performs a clearly defined role within the broader system. 
A useful principle is that enterprise AI architecture should be designed around capabilities rather than technologies. Users do not care 
whether a solution uses Azure AI Search, Azure OpenAI, or Azure API Management. They care about outcomes such as finding 
information, automating processes, improving decisions, or enhancing customer experiences. The architect's responsibility is 
therefore to design capabilities that support these outcomes while selecting technologies that implement those capabilities effectively. 
The journey begins at the user interaction layer. Every enterprise AI system ultimately exists to serve people, applications, or business 
processes. Users may access the platform through web applications, mobile applications, Microsoft Teams, business portals, APIs, or 
other channels. Although these interfaces may appear different, they all represent entry points into the same underlying architecture. 
The objective is to provide a consistent

ness portals, APIs, or 
other channels. Although these interfaces may appear different, they all represent entry points into the same underlying architecture. 
The objective is to provide a consistent and secure experience regardless of how users interact with the platform. 
Immediately behind the user interface lies the identity and security layer. Security is not an optional enhancement added later in the 
development process. It is a foundational architectural requirement. Enterprise AI systems frequently process sensitive information 
including customer records, healthcare data, financial transactions, intellectual property, and operational knowledge. Organisations 
must therefore establish strong authentication, authorisation, and access-control mechanisms before allowing users to interact with 
AI capabilities. 
Within Azure environments, services such as Microsoft Entra ID, role-based access control, managed identities, and Azure Key Vault 
help enforce these controls. The purpose of these services is not simply to protect infrastructure. They ensure that users, applications, 
and services can access only the information and capabilities they are authorised to use. Security

se of these services is not simply to protect infrastructure. They ensure that users, applications, 
and services can access only the information and capabilities they are authorised to use. Security therefore becomes an integral part 
of the architecture rather than a separate operational concern. 
Once requests have been authenticated and authorised, they typically pass through an API and access management layer. This layer 
acts as a controlled gateway between users and backend services. In large organisations, hundreds of applications and services may 
interact with AI capabilities. Without a structured access layer, governance becomes difficult and operational visibility decreases. API 
management provides a central location for authentication, traffic control, monitoring, auditing, and policy enforcement. It effectively 
acts as the front door to the AI platform. 
Beyond access management lies one of the most important components of modern enterprise AI systems: the orchestration layer. As 
AI solutions become more sophisticated, they often involve multiple models, tools, retrieval mechanisms, workflows, and business 
systems. Orchestration coordinates these components and de

layer. As 
AI solutions become more sophisticated, they often involve multiple models, tools, retrieval mechanisms, workflows, and business 
systems. Orchestration coordinates these components and determines how they interact. Rather than allowing each service to operate 
independently, orchestration provides structure and control. 
The flight booking platform developed earlier in this handbook provides a useful example. A customer request may require retrieval 
of travel policies, access to airline APIs, itinerary comparison, cost analysis, recommendation generation, and booking confirmation. 
These activities involve multiple systems and decision points. An orchestration layer coordinates the process, ensuring that each step 
occurs in the correct sequence and that information flows appropriately between components. 
The increasing popularity of agentic AI further emphasises the importance of orchestration. Agent frameworks, workflow engines, and 
state-management services enable organisations to coordinate complex activities involving planning, reasoning, retrieval, tool usage, 
and approval workflows. However, an important architectural principle remains: orchestration should

ganisations to coordinate complex activities involving planning, reasoning, retrieval, tool usage, 
and approval workflows. However, an important architectural principle remains: orchestration should remain separate from reasoning. 
The orchestration layer manages workflows and execution, while AI models focus on reasoning and generation. This separation 
improves reliability, governance, and maintainability. 
Knowledge management forms another critical architectural layer. Enterprise AI systems derive much of their value from their ability 
to access organisational knowledge. Policies, procedures, technical documentation, operational guidance, regulatory requirements, 
and business records provide the context required for informed decision making. Retrieval-Augmented Generation architectures rely 
heavily on this capability. 
Azure AI Search, vector search technologies, hybrid retrieval mechanisms, knowledge graphs, and enterprise repositories collectively 
support this layer. The objective is not merely to store information but to make it discoverable, relevant, and accessible when needed. 
Knowledge retrieval transforms static organisational information into an active asset that

The quality of this knowledge layer has a direct impact on overall AI performance. Even the most advanced language models cannot

At the centre of the platform lies the AI and model layer. This is the component most people associate with enterprise AI because it 
contains the language models responsible for reasoning, summarisation, classification, extraction, generation, and decision support. 
However, mature architectures recognise that models are only one part of a larger system. 
Modern enterprise environments often use multiple models rather than relying on a single AI capability. Different models may be

Modern enterprise environments often use multiple models rather than relying on a single AI capability. Different models may be

handled by smaller models, while others may require advanced reasoning capabilities. Model routing allows the architecture to select 
the most appropriate model dynamically, improving both cost efficiency and operational flexibility.

Chapter 9 – Enterprise Data Governance & Knowledge Ownership 
Version 2 Narrative Edition 
As organisations expand their use of artificial intelligence, they often focus on models, agents, orchestration frameworks, and user 
interfaces. While these components are important, they are rarely the primary determinants of long-term success. In practice, the 
effectiveness of enterprise AI is heavily influenced by the quality, governance, ownership, and management of the information on 
which it depends. An advanced AI system cannot consistently produce reliable outcomes if the underlying data and knowledge assets 
are incomplete, inaccurate, outdated, inconsistent, or poorly governed. 
This reality makes data governance and knowledge ownership fundamental pillars of enterprise AI. In many respects, AI amplifies the 
strengths and weaknesses of organisational knowledge. High-quality information can significantly improve decision making,

automation, and operational efficiency. Poor-quality information can lead to incorrect recommendations, reduced trust, compliance 
issues, and operational risk. For this reason, successful organisations treat data and knowledge as strategic assets rather than technical 
resources. 
A useful principle is that AI does not create knowledge; it consumes, interprets, and applies knowledge. The quality of AI outputs 
therefore depends heavily on the quality of the information being provided to the system. If organisational knowledge is fragmented, 
duplicated, outdated, or inconsistent, AI systems will inevitably reflect those problems. Governance exists to ensure that information 
remains trustworthy, accessible, and aligned with organisational objectives. 
The concept of data governance refers to the framework of policies, processes, responsibilities, and controls that determine how data 
is managed throughout its lifecycle. Governance defines who owns information, who can access it, how

significantly increased its visibility because AI systems rely on large volumes of information to generate recommendations and support 
decisions. 
Knowledge governance extends these principles beyond structured data. Enterprise AI systems frequently depend on policies, 
procedures, manuals, standards, reports, operational guidance, regulations, technical documentation, and other forms of 
unstructured information. This knowledge often resides across multiple repositories and departments, making ownership and

One of the most common challenges encountered in enterprise environments is uncertainty regarding ownership. Organisations 
frequently possess vast quantities of information, yet no clear responsibility exists for maintaining it. Documents remain unchanged 
for years. Policies become outdated. Multiple versions of the same information coexist. Different departments maintain conflicting 
guidance. When AI systems retrieve and use this information, inconsistencies become visible to users and confidence begins to erode. 
This highlights the importance of knowledge ownership. Every important information asset should have an identified owner who is 
accountable for its accuracy, relevance, and maintenance. Ownership does not necessarily imply technical responsibility. Rather, it 
reflects business accountability for the content itself. Knowledge owners understand the subject matter, determine when updates are 
required, and ensure that information remains aligned with organisational requirements.

Many organisations successfully build AI prototypes. Demonstrations work well, stakeholders become excited, and early results appear 
promising. Yet a significant number of AI initiatives struggle when moving from pilot environments into production. The reason is 
rarely the model itself. More often, organisations discover that building an AI solution and operating an AI solution are fundamentally 
different challenges. Development focuses on creating capability. Production focuses on sustaining capability reliably, securely, 
efficiently, and consistently over time. 
This distinction lies at the heart of the Production AI Operating Model. An operating model defines how AI systems are managed, 
governed, maintained, monitored, and improved throughout their operational lifecycle. It establishes the people, processes,

A useful principle is that deployment is not the end of an AI project. Deployment is the beginning of an operational journey. Once an 
AI solution enters production, it becomes part of the organisation's day-to-day activities. Users depend on it, business processes 
interact with it, governance requirements apply to it, and operational teams become responsible for supporting it. Success therefore 
depends not only on what the system can do but also on how effectively it can be managed.

Chapter 1 – AI Economics & Cost Optimisation 
As organisations move from experimentation to production deployment, one of the first challenges they encounter is cost. During 
proof-of-concept development, teams often focus primarily on functionality, accuracy, and user experience. Large language models 
are connected, prompts are created, retrieval pipelines are implemented, and initial demonstrations are produced successfully. 
However, once the solution begins serving hundreds, thousands, or millions of users, operational cost becomes a ma

val pipelines are implemented, and initial demonstrations are produced successfully. 
However, once the solution begins serving hundreds, thousands, or millions of users, operational cost becomes a major architectural 
concern. 
A common misconception is that AI cost is driven only by the model itself. In reality, enterprise AI systems incur costs across the entire 
architecture. Expenses may originate from model inference, embeddings generation, vector databases, storage, orchestration services, 
API calls, observability platforms, cloud infrastructure, networking, security controls, and operational support. A mature architect 
therefore considers cost as a system-level concern rather than a model-level concern. 
The objective of AI cost optimisation is not simply to minimise spending. Excessive cost reduction can degrade accuracy, reliability, 
and user experience. The goal is to maximise business value for every unit of cost incurred. The architect must therefore balance 
performance, quality, scalability, responsiveness, governance, and operational efficiency. 
A useful principle is that AI systems should be designed to use the most economical capability that can successfully p

e, quality, scalability, responsiveness, governance, and operational efficiency. 
A useful principle is that AI systems should be designed to use the most economical capability that can successfully perform the 
required task. Not every request requires the most powerful model. Not every query requires retrieval. Not every response requires 
generation. Understanding this principle is central to enterprise AI economics. 
 
Understanding Enterprise AI Costs 
Before optimisation can occur, organisations must understand where money is being spent. In most enterprise AI systems, costs 
generally fall into several categories. 
The first category is model inference cost. This is usually the most visible cost and includes charges associated with generating 
responses from large language models such as GPT, Claude, Gemini, or hosted open-source models. 
The second category is retrieval cost. This includes vector database queries, graph traversals, search operations, reranking models, 
and context assembly. 
The third category is embedding cost. Every document that enters the knowledge system may require embedding generation. Large 
enterprise repositories can therefore generate substantial

chunks.source_document: Enterprise AI Consulting & Production Readiness Handbook.pdf
chunks.source_text_file: Enterprise AI Consulting & Production Readiness Handbook.txt
chunks.text:
ext assembly. 
The third category is embedding cost. Every document that enters the knowledge system may require embedding generation. Large 
enterprise repositories can therefore generate substantial embedding expenses. 
The fourth category is infrastructure cost. This includes cloud compute, storage, networking, monitoring systems, Kubernetes clusters, 
GPU resources, and operational services. 
The fifth category is engineering and operational cost. This includes development, testing, evaluation, governance, maintenance, 
support, and continuous improvement activities. 
A consultant should therefore avoid focusing solely on model cost because optimisation opportunities exist across the entire 
architecture. 
 
The Cost-Performance Trade-Off 
Every AI architecture operates within a triangle consisting of quality, speed, and cost. 
 
 
 
Improving quality often increases cost. Improving speed may require additional infrastructure. Reducing cost may affect response 
quality. 
The architect's responsibility is to determine the most appropriate balance based on business objectives. 
For example, an airline booking assistant may require highly accurate policy interpretation because inc

chunks.source_document: Enterprise AI Consulting & Production Readiness Handbook.pdf
chunks.source_text_file: Enterprise AI Consulting & Production Readiness Handbook.txt

ect's responsibility is to determine the most appropriate balance based on business objectives. 
For example, an airline booking assistant may require highly accurate policy interpretation because incorrect answers could affect 
bookings and customer satisfaction. In this case, slightly higher AI costs may be justified. Conversely, a simple FAQ chatbot may not 
require premium models for every interaction. 
The correct answer therefore depends on business value rather than technical preference. 
 
Scenario: GPT Cost Is Too High 
One of the most common enterprise questions is: 
GPT cost is too high. What should we do? 
A mature consultant does not immediately replace GPT with a cheaper model. Instead, they investigate the architecture systematically. 
The first question is whether GPT is being used efficiently. 


[Page 38]
The second question is whether every request genuinely requires GPT. 
The third question is whether the architecture can reduce unnecessary model usage. 
Only after these questions have been explored should model replacement be considered.

Cost Optimisation in the Flight Booking Platform 
The flight booking platform provides a practical example. 
A cost-efficient architecture may apply several optimisation layers simultaneously. 
Frequently asked questions are served from cache. Simple informational requests are routed to smaller models. Premium GPT models 
are reserved for complex travel planning and policy interpretation. Retrieval systems return only the most relevant documents. 
Embeddings are regenerated only when knowledge sources change. Deterministic APIs handle booking status, flight availability, 
payment confirmation, and itinerary retrieval. 
The result is a layered architecture that delivers high-quality responses while controlling operational expenditure.

Business Perspective 
From a consulting perspective, the objective is not to build the cheapest system. 
The objective is to build the most economically effective system. 
An expensive AI system may be justified if it generates significantly greater business value. Conversely, even a relatively inexpensive 
system may be unacceptable if it fails to produce measurable outcomes. 
A consultant therefore evaluates cost in relation to business benefit. 
The central question becomes: 
Does the value generated by the AI system justify the resources consumed? 
When this principle guides architectural decisions, cost optimisation becomes an enabler of business success rather than merely a

Chapter 2 – Stakeholder Management & Decision Making 
Technology projects rarely fail because the technology itself is impossible to implement. More often, projects fail because stakeholders 
have different objectives, priorities, expectations, constraints, and definitions of success. One group may prioritise speed, another 
may prioritise governance, another may prioritise cost reduction, while another may focus on user experience. The consultant's 
responsibility is therefore not only to design systems but also to align people. 
Stakeholder management is the process o

responsibility is therefore not only to design systems but also to align people. 
Stakeholder management is the process of understanding who is affected by a project, what they want to achieve, how they influence 
decisions, and how conflicts between competing priorities can be resolved. In many consulting engagements, stakeholder alignment 
is often more challenging than the technical implementation itself. 
A useful principle is that every stakeholder views the project through a different lens. Business leaders focus on value and return on 
investment. Operational teams focus on workflow efficiency and reliability. Compliance teams focus on governance and regulatory 
obligations. Security teams focus on risk. Engineering teams focus on feasibility, scalability, and maintainability. End users focus on 
usability and outcomes. 
The consultant must therefore understand not only the solution but also the people involved in the solution.

One of the most common mistakes made by technical teams is focusing on what can be built rather than what should be built. 
Engineers are naturally attracted to interesting technologies, advanced architectures, powerful models, and sophisticated features. 
However, organisations do not invest in AI because it is technically impressive. They invest because they expect measurable business 
outcomes. 
AI product thinking is the discipline of connecting user needs, business objectives, operational constraints, and technological 
capabilities into a coherent product strategy. It ensures that development effort is directed toward features that c

capabilities into a coherent product strategy. It ensures that development effort is directed toward features that create value rather 
than features that simply demonstrate technical sophistication. 
A useful principle is that users do not buy AI. They buy solutions to problems. The user rarely cares whether the underlying 
implementation uses GPT, Claude, RAG, agents, vector databases, or knowledge graphs. What matters is whether the solution helps 
them complete tasks more effectively, more efficiently, more accurately, or with less effort. 
For this reason, successful AI products begin with user needs rather than model selection.

Understanding User Needs 
Every successful product starts with understanding users. 
Questions include:

•

•

• 
What causes errors? 
• 
What prevents adoption? 
The objective is to identify genuine user problems rather than assumed problems. 
Many organisations discover that the solution users actually need differs significantly from the solution originally proposed. 
 
User Value Proposition 
A useful question is: 
Why would somebody use this product? 
 
If the answer is unclear, the product is unlikely to succeed.

User Trust and Adoption 
An AI product provides little value if users do not trust it. 
Adoption depends on several factors. 
Users must understand what the system does. 
Users must understand its limitations. 
Users must believe recommendations are reliable. 
Users must experience consistent value. 
Trust therefore becomes a product feature. 
In many cases, explainability, transparency, and accuracy contribute more to adoption than additional functionality. 
 
Measuring Product Success 
Product success should be measurable. 
Questions include: 
Are users adopting the product? 
 
Are they returning? 
 
Are business outcomes improving? 
 
Are support costs decreasing? 
 
Is productivity increasing? 
 
Is customer satisfaction improving?

Metrics should align with the original business objectives.

Common Product Mistakes 
Several patterns repeatedly appear in unsuccessful AI products. 
Some organisations prioritise technology over user needs. 
Others attempt to build too many features at once. 
Some focus on model performance while ignoring adoption. 
Others optimise for demonstrations rather than real-world usage. 
Many products fail because they solve interesting problems rather than important problems. 
The consultant should remain vigilant against these tendencies.

Product thinking bridges the gap between business strategy and technical implementation. 
Without product thinking, teams risk building technically impressive solutions that generate little value. 
With product thinking, technology becomes a tool for solving meaningful problems and creating measurable outcomes. 
The consultant's role is therefore not simply to identify what can be built but to determine what should be built, when it should be

Chapter 4: 
Chapter 4 – Responsible AI & Trustworthy Systems 
As artificial intelligence becomes increasingly integrated into business operations, decision-making processes, customer interactions, 
and enterprise workflows, organisations face an important challenge. Building an AI system that functions correctly is no longer 
sufficient. Organisations must also ensure that the system behaves responsibly, remains trustworthy, complies with regulations, and 
continues to deserve user confidence over time. 
Responsible AI is the discipline of designing, deploying, operating, and governing AI systems in a manner that is safe, transparent, 
explainable, fair, secure, accountable, and aligned with organisational values. Trustworthy AI extends this concept by focusing on 
whether users, regulators, stakeholders, and organisations can rely on AI outputs with confidence. 
The objective is not simply to create intelligent systems. The objective is to create systems that peopl

chunks.source_document: Enterprise AI Consulting & Production Readiness Handbook.pdf

chunks.text:
her users, regulators, stakeholders, and organisations can rely on AI outputs with confidence. 
The objective is not simply to create intelligent systems. The objective is to create systems that people are willing to use, trust, adopt, 
and govern. 
A useful principle is that technical capability and trustworthiness are independent. A highly capable AI system may still be unacceptable 
if it produces biased recommendations, exposes sensitive information, cannot explain its decisions, or operates without accountability. 
 
Why Responsible AI Matters 
Historically, many software systems performed deterministic calculations. If a system produced an incorrect result, engineers could 
often trace the logic and identify the cause relatively easily. 
Modern AI systems behave differently. 
Large language models generate probabilistic outputs. Recommendation systems make predictions. Forecasting systems estimate 
future events. Agentic systems make decisions about tool usage and workflow execution. 
As AI autonomy increases, governance requirements also increase. 
Organisations therefore need mechanisms to ensure that AI systems remain aligned with business objectives, legal obligations, et

ion. 
As AI autonomy increases, governance requirements also increase. 
Organisations therefore need mechanisms to ensure that AI systems remain aligned with business objectives, legal obligations, ethical 
standards, and stakeholder expectations. 
Responsible AI is ultimately about risk management and trust preservation. 
 
The Pillars of Responsible AI 
A useful framework consists of six major pillars. 
Fairness 
Transparency 
Explainability 
Accountability 
Security 
Human Oversight 
These pillars appear repeatedly across government frameworks, enterprise AI policies, industry standards, and regulatory guidance. 
Although terminology varies, the underlying concepts remain largely consistent. 
 
Fairness 
Fairness addresses the question: 
Is the AI treating people, situations, or groups appropriately? 
AI systems learn from data. If historical data contains imbalances, incomplete representation, or unintended biases, those 
characteristics may influence model behaviour. 
Consider an NHS appointment recommendation system. 
Suppose the system consistently recommends appointments in certain geographical regions because those regions historically had 
more available capacity. 
The mo

more available capacity. 
The model may unintentionally disadvantage patients in other areas. 
Similarly, in a transportation platform, a matching system may consistently prioritise certain drivers due to historical patterns. 
The concern is not whether outcomes differ. The concern is whether those differences are unjustified, harmful, discriminatory, or 
inconsistent with organisational objectives. 
Responsible AI therefore requires continuous monitoring and evaluation of outcomes.

The objective is not merely to provide an answer but to provide understandable reasoning.

Responsible AI in the Flight Booking Platform 
The flight booking platform provides a useful example. 
Fairness requires that recommendations are not manipulated unfairly toward specific providers without legitimate business 
justification. 
Transparency requires that users understand when AI-generated recommendations are being provided. 
Explainability requires that route recommendations, policy interpretations, and booking suggestions can be justified. 
Accountability ensures that the organisation remains responsible for information presented to customers. 
Security protects customer information, payment processes, and travel data. 
Human oversight may be required when unusual circumstances arise or when confidence levels are low. 
Together, these controls help create a trustworthy customer experience.

Chapter 5: 
Chapter 5 – Advanced Agent Design Patterns 
As enterprise AI systems mature, organisations often move beyond simple chatbot interactions and begin building more sophisticated 
workflows that involve planning, reasoning, tool usage, decision-making, retrieval, validation, and execution.

workflows that involve planning, reasoning, tool usage, decision-making, retrieval, validation, and execution. This evolution introduces 
the concept of AI agents. 
An AI agent is a system that can perceive information, reason about objectives, select actions, use tools, interact with external systems, 
and work toward completing a task. Unlike a traditional chatbot that simply responds to a prompt, an agent can perform multiple 
coordinated activities before producing a result. 
However, many organisations mistakenly assume that every AI problem requires agents. In reality, agents introduce complexity, cost, 
governance challenges, and operational risk. A mature architect therefore treats agents as a design pattern rather than a default 
solution. 
The objective is not to maximise agent usage. The objective is to use agents only when they create meaningful value.

Several mistakes frequently appear in enterprise projects. 
Some organisations introduce agents when deterministic workflows would be simpler.

Chapter 6 – Retrieval Evaluation & RAG Quality Frameworks 
One of the most common misconceptions in enterprise AI is that improving the language model automatically improves the quality of 
the overall system. In reality, many failures in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems originate not from the model but from 
retrieval itself. If the wrong information is retrieved, even the most capable model will struggle to generate a correct answer. 
Conversely, if highly relevant information is retrieved, even a smaller model may produce excellent r

chunks.source_document: Enterprise AI Consulting & Production Readiness Handbook.pdf
chunks.source_text_file: Enterprise AI Consulting & Production Readiness Handbook.txt
chunks.text:
information is retrieved, even the most capable model will struggle to generate a correct answer. 
Conversely, if highly relevant information is retrieved, even a smaller model may produce excellent results. 
For this reason, mature organisations evaluate retrieval separately from generation. Rather than asking whether the answer was good, 
they ask a sequence of more detailed questions. Was the correct information available? Was the correct information retrieved? Was 
the retrieved information relevant? Did the model use the information correctly? Did the final answer satisfy the user? 
This approach transforms evaluation from a vague judgement into a structured engineering discipline. 
A useful principle is that every RAG system consists of at least four major stages. 
Knowledge 
↓ 
Retrieval 
↓ 
Generation 
↓ 
User 
Outcome 
 
Each stage should be evaluated independently before evaluating the system as a whole. 
 
Why Retrieval Evaluation Matters 
Consider the flight booking platform. 
A user asks: 
Can I take two checked bags on this route? 
Suppose the airline policy repository contains the correct answer. 
However, the retrieval system returns an outdated baggage policy. 
The

The problem is not the model. 
The problem is retrieval. 
Now consider a second scenario. 
The correct policy is retrieved, but the model misinterprets it. 
In this case, retrieval succeeded but generation failed. 
Without structured evaluation, these two failures may appear identical. 
Retrieval evaluation therefore allows organisations to identify where problems actually originate.

Chapter 7 – Human Evaluation, Adoption & Feedback Systems 
One of the most common mistakes made in AI projects is assuming that a technically successful system is automatically a successful 
product. Teams often spend months optimising prompts, improving retrieval quality, refining agents, increasing model accuracy, and 
reducing hallucinations. While these activities are important, they do not guarantee that users will trust t

reducing hallucinations. While these activities are important, they do not guarantee that users will trust the system, adopt it, or derive 
value from it. 
A useful principle is that users do not experience metrics. Users experience outcomes. They care whether the system helps them solve 
problems, save time, improve decisions, reduce effort, or achieve objectives. As a result, human evaluation becomes just as important 
as technical evaluation. 
Technical evaluation tells us whether the system works correctly. Human evaluation tells us whether the system works usefully. 
Successful enterprise AI therefore requires both.

Consider the flight booking platform. 
Suppose retrieval accuracy is excellent.

Hallucination rates are low. 
Latency is acceptable. 
From a technical perspective, the system appears successful.

Perhaps explanations are too long. 
Perhaps users do not trust the recommendations.

The technical system may be performing well while the business outcome remains poor.

Chapter 9 – Enterprise Data Governance & Knowledge Ownership 
Most enterprise AI discussions focus on models, prompts, agents, retrieval systems, and cloud platforms. However, many AI failures 
originate from a much simpler problem: organisations do not know who owns their data, where information comes from, whether it 
is trustworthy, or who is responsible for maintaining it. 
A useful principle is that AI systems do not create knowledge. They consume knowledge. The quality, reliability, and trustworthiness 
of an AI system are therefore fundamentally constrained by the quality o

ul principle is that AI systems do not create knowledge. They consume knowledge. The quality, reliability, and trustworthiness 
of an AI system are therefore fundamentally constrained by the quality of the information it receives. 
For this reason, data governance and knowledge ownership are among the most important foundations of enterprise AI. Organisations 
that manage knowledge effectively can often achieve strong AI outcomes with relatively simple models. Organisations with poor 
governance frequently struggle even when using the most advanced AI technologies. 
The objective is not simply to store information. The objective is to ensure that information remains accurate, current, secure, 
traceable, governed, and owned throughout its lifecycle. 
 
Why Data Governance Matters 
Consider the flight booking platform. 
Suppose the system recommends a baggage allowance based on outdated airline documentation. 
The language model may perform perfectly. 
The retrieval system may function correctly. 
The orchestration workflow may execute successfully. 
The answer may still be wrong. 
The root cause is neither the model nor the architecture. 
The root cause is knowledge governance.

Chapter 10 – Production AI Operating Model 
Many organisations successfully build AI prototypes. Far fewer successfully operate AI systems in production. The transition from 
proof-of-concept to enterprise operation introduces new challenges involving reliability, governance, monitoring, deployment, 
security, evaluation, incident management, cost control, change management, and organisational ownership. 
A useful principle is that building an AI system and operating an AI system are fundamentally different activities. During development, 
the focus is on experimentation, learning, and capability creation. During production, the focus shifts toward reliability, predictability, 
governance, scalability, and continuous improvement. 
The objective of a Production AI Operating Model is to define how AI systems are managed throughout their operational lifecycle. It 


[Page 98]
establishes the people, processes, technologies, governance mechanisms, and operational practices required to sustain AI at 
enterprise scale. 
Without an operating model, organisations often experience inconsistent deployments, uncontrolled cos

es, governance mechanisms, and operational practices required to sustain AI at 
enterprise scale. 
Without an operating model, organisations often experience inconsistent deployments, uncontrolled costs, governance gaps, 
declining quality, and reduced trust.

The initial prototype may work well during demonstrations. 
The retrieval system may function correctly.

These questions are operational rather than technical. 
An operating model provides the answers.

AI Product Thinking & Roadmap Prioritisation 
What would you build first? 
I would build the smallest solution that delivers measurable business value and allows us to validate key assumptions. I would prioritise 
a high-value, low-complexity use case that can be delivered quickly and provide evidence for future investment. 
 
How would you prioritise AI initiatives? 
I would evaluate each initiative against business value, customer impact, strategic alignment, technical feasibility, data availability, 
risk, implementation effort, and time to market. High-value initiatives with manageable complexity would typically be prioritised first. 
 
How do you define an MVP? 
An MVP is the smallest solution capable of delivering meaningful value while enabling learning. Its purpose is to validate assumptions, 
gather feedback, reduce uncertainty, and provide evidence before investing in larger-scale development. 
 
How would you decide whether a use

chunks.source_document: Enterprise AI Consulting & Production Readiness Handbook.pdf
chunks.source_text_file: Enterprise AI Consulting & Production Readiness Handbook.txt
chunks.text:
abling learning. Its purpose is to validate assumptions, 
gather feedback, reduce uncertainty, and provide evidence before investing in larger-scale development. 
 
How would you decide whether a use case is worth funding? 
I would assess the business problem, expected benefits, implementation cost, operational cost, risk, data availability, organisational 
readiness, and measurable success criteria. A use case is worth funding when the expected value significantly exceeds the investment 
and risk involved. 
 
How do you balance value against complexity? 
I focus on achieving the highest possible business value with the lowest reasonable complexity. If two solutions deliver similar value, 
I would usually choose the simpler one because it is cheaper, faster to deliver, easier to govern, and easier to maintain. 
 
Responsible AI 
How would you prevent bias? 
I would assess training and knowledge sources, evaluate outcomes across different groups, monitor behaviour continuously, and 
involve domain experts in reviewing recommendations and decisions. 
 
How would you ensure explainability? 
I would provide evidence, reasoning, citations, confidence indicators, and clear explanations s

How would you ensure explainability? 
I would provide evidence, reasoning, citations, confidence indicators, and clear explanations showing why recommendations were 
generated. 
 
How would you maintain accountability? 
I would establish clear ownership for data, models, prompts, decisions, and operational outcomes, ensuring that humans remain 
accountable for AI-assisted decisions. 
 
What role should humans play? 
Humans should define objectives, approve high-risk actions, review critical decisions, oversee governance, and intervene when 
confidence is low or consequences are significant. 
 
How would you prevent misuse? 
I would implement authentication, authorisation, content filtering, audit logging, monitoring, policy enforcement, prompt injection 
protection, and human approval workflows where appropriate. 
 
How would you build trust? 
By delivering accurate, explainable, consistent, secure, and transparent outcomes while maintaining governance, human oversight, 
and continuous evaluation.

Agent Design Patterns 
For a complex enterprise system, in what order are these typically called? 
A common enterprise sequence is: 
User Request 
        ↓ 
Router Agent 
        ↓ 
Planner Agent 
        ↓ 
Executor Agents 
        ↓ 
Reviewer Agent 
        ↓ 
Critic Agent (optional) 
        ↓ 
Human Approval Agent (if required) 
        ↓ 
Supervisor Agent 
        ↓ 
Final Response / Action 
 
The Supervisor may monitor the workflow throughout rather than appearing only at the end. 
 
When would you use agents? 
When a problem requires reasoning, planning, adaptation, tool usage, coordination, or multi-step decision making. 
 
Why not use a workflow? 
If the process is deterministic, predictable, and well-defined, a traditi

requires reasoning, planning, adaptation, tool usage, coordination, or multi-step decision making. 
 
Why not use a workflow? 
If the process is deterministic, predictable, and well-defined, a traditional workflow is usually simpler, cheaper, more reliable, and 
easier to govern. 
 
When does multi-agent become appropriate? 
When specialised expertise, parallel activities, complex planning, or coordination between multiple responsibilities is required. 
 
What is the role of a planner? 
The planner decomposes a complex objective into smaller tasks and determines the sequence required to achieve the goal. 
 
How would you govern agents? 
Using role boundaries, approval workflows, audit logs, policy enforcement, observability, access controls, and continuous evaluation.
